items.title: Score held-out data only
items.avoid: Computing RMSE/MAE on the same rows used to fit parameters.
items.prefer: Fit on train; compute validation RMSE for tuning; report test RMSE/MAE once for final evaluation.
items.why: Training metrics underestimate generalization error and mislead model comparison.
items.example: LinearRegression.fit(X_train, y_train); metrics only on model.predict(X_test) vs y_test.

items.title: Report metrics in target units
items.avoid: Publishing MSE in squared dollars without converting to RMSE.
items.prefer: Report RMSE or MAE so stakeholders read error in the same units as y.
items.why: Squared units (MSE) are hard to interpret; RMSE restores linear scale.
items.example: House prices: say "test RMSE $48k" not "test MSE 2.3 billion dollar²".

items.title: Compare against a baseline
items.prefer: Report baseline RMSE (mean predictor, last-value forecast) on the same test split.
items.why: Absolute RMSE/MAE alone does not reveal whether the model beats trivial predictors.
items.example: Model RMSE 50 vs mean-baseline RMSE 55 → modest win. Baseline 30 → model fails.

items.title: Use both RMSE and MAE when errors are skewed
items.avoid: Relying on a single number when residuals have heavy tails.
items.prefer: Publish test MAE for typical error and test RMSE for tail sensitivity; inspect residual plots.
items.why: A large RMSE–MAE gap signals a few dominant outliers worth investigating.
items.example: MAE = 2°C, RMSE = 8°C → investigate the 1% of predictions with huge misses.
